title: Landing Chains
desc: The draw platforms at the reservoir edge are running rough, and rough equipment at black water is equipment asking for attention.

steps.text:
Water does not climb out of Mount Pillar Reservoir by goodwill. It comes up by draw platforms, chain-guided frames, and creatures willing to lean over black water while pretending that is an ordinary way to spend the morning. The lower landing on the east side has started dragging at the guide track, and the steps down to it are slick enough that even Danians have begun commenting on them.

The landing keeper checks the chain with one hand and the stone steps with the other. "We can oil the guide run and replace the worn pins," she says. "Or scrape the landing stairs and cleats before someone brings up a full bucket rack and forgets what traction is. Both jobs prevent explanations later."

options.label: Service the chain guides and replace the worn pins
Scrape the landing stairs and cleats for footing

steps.text:
The chain guide is not damaged, only tired. Three pins have worn enough to wobble in their housings, and the whole run is dry where the last oiling should have happened yesterday instead of becoming today's problem. You knock out the bad pins, seat fresh ones from the keeper's tray, and work new oil into the moving links until the platform begins traveling with a steady metallic complaint instead of the rough grinding it started with.

When the keeper lowers and raises the empty frame again, it tracks smooth against the side rail, no catch, no jump, no alarming hesitation at the midpoint. She listens to one full cycle and seems satisfied. "Good. Machinery is allowed to sound old. It is not allowed to sound uncertain." At reservoir edge, that passes for philosophy.

options.label: Cap the oil pot and clear the frame for use

steps.text:
The landing stairs are cut directly into damp stone and would be treacherous even without the fine algae film that reservoir spray keeps painting over them. You scrape each tread clean, rough the cleats with a stone file, and cut channels through the green slick until the steps feel like stairs again instead of a suggestion made by water.

By the time you finish, the difference can be heard as much as seen. Boots land with grip instead of skid. The first empty rack crew coming down the steps does not have to touch the wall once. The keeper watches them go, then looks at the cleaned treads with something like respect. "There. Now if anyone falls in, it will have to be from carelessness instead of poor maintenance." That is as generous as she gets.

options.label: Pile the scraped slime for disposal and reopen the landing
